summary: My name is Kyoichi Fujimoto, born in the 30th year of Meiji, 1897, in Yamaguchi Prefecture. I came to America alone at age 15, arriving in San Francisco during President Taft's visit. I lived in Stockton with Mr. Jinnojo Hayashi and Mr. Makoto Kobayashi, attending school and delivering Japanese newspapers. My father had been in Stockton for years working in asparagus fields, but I grew up apart from my parents, raised by my grandmother.

I was born in the 30th year of Meiji, which corresponds to 1897. Growing up during the Meiji era in Yamaguchi Prefecture, I was too young to recall much about life in Japan before I left at age 15. The Meiji period was a time of change, but my memories mostly start from my childhood there before emigrating.

I was born in Yamaguchi Prefecture in 1897 during the Meiji era. It was my home until I left for America at age 15. My father and others from the same area later settled in Stockton, California, where they worked in agriculture.

Stockton was where my father lived and worked when I arrived in America. I stayed with Mr. Jinnojo Hayashi there, who was an agent for the Nichibei Daily and involved in asparagus farming. Stockton had a growing Japanese community of about 6,000 people, many working down the river in agriculture. I went to school there and delivered Japanese newspapers while living in town.

Mr. Jinnojo Hayashi was a key figure in Stockton's Japanese community and a guardian to me when I arrived in America. He was an agent for the Nichibei Daily newspaper and also engaged in asparagus farming down the river. I lived with him in Stockton at 121 East Rafaet, where the newspaper office and a picture studio were located. He was well respected and helped many Japanese immigrants settle here.

I arrived in San Francisco by ship in the fall when President William Howard Taft was visiting the city, which caused much commotion. My father was unable to meet me at the port, so I was sent to Angel Island for immigration processing. I stayed only two days in San Francisco before traveling on to Stockton. The city was still recovering from the big earthquake and had few automobiles, but I was impressed by the harbor and ferries.

When I arrived in San Francisco, President William Howard Taft was visiting the city, which caused many offices to close and a big celebration. Because of his visit, I was delayed in entering the city and had to stay on Angel Island for about two weeks. The President's visit was a notable event during my arrival in America.

After arriving in San Francisco, I was sent to Angel Island's immigration office because my father couldn't meet me at the port. I stayed there about two weeks with a group of stowaways, struggling with the food and environment. Eventually, when President Taft's visit ended, my father came to Angel Island and I was allowed to enter San Francisco.

Mr. Kinji Ushijima was a pioneer in Stockton's Japanese agricultural community. Along with Mr. Matsumoto, he found a Caucasian financier and helped dredge fertile soil from the river to create islands for farming. His efforts made farming down the river very successful and helped establish the Japanese immigrant community in Stockton.

My father worked alongside Mr. Renjutsu Matsusita and Mr. Jinnojo Hayashi in Stockton. They all came from the same area in Japan and were involved in asparagus farming on Orlude Island in the San Joaquin River. Mr. Matsusita was well educated and respected in the community.

Orlude Island is located in the San Joaquin River and was where my father worked in asparagus fields. I stayed there briefly after arriving in America before moving into town to attend school. The island still produces asparagus today and had a ferry service connecting it to the mainland and other islands.

The San Joaquin River area was central to the Japanese farming community where my father and others worked. Islands created by dredging fertile soil in the river, like Orlude Island and Shima Tract, became important agricultural sites. Many Japanese immigrants lived and farmed along the river and its islands.

The Nichibei Daily was a Japanese newspaper published in Stockton where I worked delivering papers. Mr. Jinnojo Hayashi was the agent for the paper, and the office was located at 121 East Rafaet. The paper had a strong readership in the community, with about eighty copies delivered daily in town and to nearby islands.

Mr. Niiyama ran a picture studio on the second floor of the building at 121 East Rafaet in Stockton, where I lived for a time. His studio was above the Nichibei Daily newspaper office. I remember his wife cooked for us, including the pastor, and I was familiar with the Presbyterian Church nearby because of living there.

The Presbyterian Church in Stockton was a small room located in the same building where I lived and worked delivering newspapers. I lived next to the church and became familiar with it because I stayed with the pastor, Mr. Makoto Kobayashi, for a while. The church was part of the growing Japanese immigrant community's efforts to organize and support each other.

I traveled from Japan to America on the ship named Tenyo-Maru, leaving from Yokohama in the fall. The port was undeveloped then, so we were ferried to the ship by rowboats. The voyage took seventeen days, and I was the only child on board going to a foreign country at that time.

I remember seeing the beautiful Mount Fuji as we left Japan on the Tenyo-Maru in the fall. It was a striking sight and one of my last memories of home before arriving in America. Mount Fuji symbolized the Japan I left behind as I began my new life abroad.

Before coming to America, I knew about George Washington from stories like 'The Cherry Tree and His Ax,' which taught about his honesty. I was aware that America was a Christian country and had some basic knowledge of its history, even though I had little opportunity to read about it. These stories shaped my early understanding of the country I was about to enter.

Yokohama was the port from which I departed Japan on the Tenyo-Maru. At that time, the port was undeveloped with no pier, so passengers were transported to the ship by rowboats. Yokohama was just a name then, but it was my gateway to America.

Mr. Makoto Kobayashi was a pastor in Stockton who acted as one of my guardians when I moved into town from Orlude Island. I lived with him for a while and became familiar with the Presbyterian Church through that relationship. He supported me as I attended school and adjusted to life in America.

The Santa Fe train station was near the pier in San Francisco where I arrived. Trains waited there to pick up passengers from incoming boats. I took a train from Santa Fe station to Orlude Island, which took about seventy minutes, and I was amazed by the vastness of the country during that journey.

The Nichibei Daily newspaper later changed its name to Hokubei Asahi, which I also helped deliver. This paper was part of the Japanese community's daily life in Stockton and the surrounding areas. Delivering these papers was part of my routine as a young immigrant.

Shinsekai was one of the Japanese newspapers I delivered in Stockton and nearby islands. Along with the Nichibei Daily and Ofu Nippo, it helped keep the Japanese community informed and connected. Delivering these papers was part of my daily work while attending school.

Ofu Nippo was a Japanese newspaper published in Sacramento that I delivered during my lunch breaks in Stockton. It was one of several papers serving the Japanese immigrant community in California. Delivering it helped me earn money while I was still in school.

Sacramento was the city where the Ofu Nippo newspaper was published, which I delivered in Stockton. Though I lived in Stockton, the Japanese community was connected across cities through newspapers like Ofu Nippo. Sacramento was part of the broader network of Japanese immigrants in California.

Mr. Matsumoto, who was a leader in Stockton's Japanese community and involved in farming and business, came from Kumamoto Prefecture. He was well educated and helped found the Shima Tract and was a stockholder in the Stockton Hotel. His background from Kumamoto connected him with other immigrants and helped build the community.

The Stockton Hotel is the oldest building in Stockton and was associated with Mr. Matsumoto, who was a stockholder there. It was part of the infrastructure supporting the Japanese immigrant community. Mr. Matsumoto's involvement in the hotel reflected his role as a community leader beyond farming.

The Shima Tract is a strip of island about seven acres in the San Joaquin River area, developed for farming by Mr. Matsumoto from Kumamoto Prefecture. It was a successful agricultural operation and remains active today. This tract was part of the efforts by Japanese immigrants to establish prosperous farming communities down the river.

Bacon Island was one of the islands in the San Joaquin River area where Japanese immigrants lived and farmed. It was part of the network of islands connected by ferry services for mail and newspaper delivery. Many Japanese laborers lived in camps on islands like Bacon Island during the early 20th century.
